title_en: Commercial, Family, and Social Traditions in At-Tamhid
title_ar: أحاديث التجارة والأسرة والمجتمع في التمهيد
content_en:
The commercial, family, and social traditions of the Muwatta receive from Ibn Abd al-Barr in At-Tamhid the same comprehensive treatment that he applies to the worship sections. The commercial traditions are analyzed for their chain, their linguistic content, and their legal implications, with Ibn Abd al-Barr presenting the positions of all four schools on the rulings they yield. The Muwatta's commercial traditions are particularly important because Imam Malik's positions on commercial law were distinctive in several respects and his chain of transmission provided access to the Medinan commercial practice of the early generations.

The family law traditions of the Muwatta — covering marriage, divorce, custody, and inheritance — receive extensive treatment in At-Tamhid. Ibn Abd al-Barr's commentary on these sections is among the most valuable resources available for understanding the early history of Islamic family law, since it traces the development of rulings from their prophetic origins through the various Companion and Successor positions to the formulation of the classical schools. His identification of the points of genuine dispute versus apparent dispute, and his assessment of the strength of evidence on each side, is consistently illuminating.

The sections on transactions involving uncertainty, prohibited foods, and the conditions of valid contracts are treated with Ibn Abd al-Barr's characteristic combination of linguistic precision and cross-school legal comparison. His discussions of the conditions under which various forms of sales and exchanges are permissible or prohibited reflect a command of both the hadith evidence and the legal tradition that few scholars in any era have matched.

The social ethics traditions of the Muwatta — covering the treatment of family, neighbors, servants, and all members of the community — receive from Ibn Abd al-Barr commentary that integrates their legal content with broader ethical reflection. His discussions of the traditions on the rights of the poor, the obligations of the wealthy, and the standards of justice in social relations draw on a profound engagement with the Quranic and prophetic vision of a just Muslim society.
